## The optimization problem

Training a machine learning model means finding the parameter values that minimize
a loss function. For linear regression with mean squared error, the loss is

$$
L(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{2n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\bigr)^2
$$

where $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b$. The factor $1/2$ is
a convention that simplifies the gradient.

## The gradient descent update rule

Gradient descent is an iterative algorithm. At each step it moves the parameters in
the direction of steepest descent:

$$
\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \eta\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} L(\mathbf{w}, b)
\qquad
b \leftarrow b - \eta\,\nabla_{b} L(\mathbf{w}, b)
$$

The learning rate $\eta$ controls the step size. Too large and the updates overshoot
the minimum; too small and convergence is impractically slow.

::: {.teacher-only}
Show the loss-surface visualization with different learning rates. Ask students to
predict what happens when $\eta$ is 10x too large before showing the divergence plot.
:::

## The role of feature scaling

When features have very different scales, the loss surface is elongated and gradient
descent oscillates. Standardizing each feature to zero mean and unit variance

$$
x_j^{\text{new}} = \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}
$$

makes the contours more circular and allows a single learning rate to work well
across all dimensions.

## Batch, stochastic, and mini-batch variants

**Batch gradient descent** computes the gradient over the entire training set before
each update. It is stable but expensive for large datasets.

**Stochastic gradient descent (SGD)** updates after each individual example. It is
noisy but much faster per iteration and can escape shallow local minima.

**Mini-batch gradient descent** is the practical compromise. It computes the gradient
over a small random subset (batch) of the data, balancing noise and computational
efficiency. Most deep learning frameworks default to mini-batch training.

## Analytical vs iterative solutions

Linear regression has a closed-form solution via the normal equations:

$$
\mathbf{w} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top \mathbf{y}
$$

This is exact but requires inverting a matrix, which becomes expensive when the
number of features is large or when $X^\top X$ is nearly singular. Gradient descent
scales better and generalizes to non-linear models where no closed-form solution
exists.
